findings.location: Section 2.6 N7 + Section 2.4 P2 + Section 2.3 I1 + Section 5 Rank 10
findings.claim: Phase 1 defers Invariant 11 (system-zone labels unreachable from user operations) to Phase 2 while simultaneously storing CapabilityGrant Nodes (P2), IVM ViewDefinition Nodes (I1 — even labeled 'system:IVMView'), and engine-internal metadata as plain Nodes in the same content-addressed store. The plan relies on 'engine-API-only paths' via N7 (Engine::grant_capability, Engine::create_view, Engine::revoke_capability) as the sole protection, but without Invariant 11 enforcement in the evaluator, nothing prevents a user-authored subgraph from issuing a WRITE primitive targeting a 'system:CapabilityGrant' labeled Node with arbitrary GRANTED_TO edges. In a NoAuthBackend deployment (the Phase 1 default) a user can forge a capability grant by registering a handler that WRITEs one. Once UCANBackend ships in Phase 3, the same forgery attack works against a deployed production instance if any user retains a WRITE capability on the graph at all, because the capability check happens on the write itself but the forged grant Node's label is indistinguishable from a legitimate engine-issued one at the storage layer. This is pre-auth privilege escalation shipped as Phase 1 foundation.
findings.evidence: Section 2.6 N7: 'Phase 1 doesn't enforce invariant 11 at the evaluator yet, but engine API should be the only way to mutate capability/view Nodes anyway.' ENGINE-SPEC.md line 227: 'The evaluator rejects any operation Node whose target resolves to a system-zone label. System-zone Nodes are only writable through dedicated engine APIs (e.g., engine.grantCapability(), engine.createView()), never through operation subgraphs directly. This prevents privilege escalation via graph mutation.' Section 2.3 I1: 'System-zone label enforcement is Phase 2 (invariant 11), so for Phase 1 the system: prefix is a convention enforced by the IVM crate, not the eval crate.' Section 5 Rank 10 lists Invariant 11 as explicit Phase 2 deferral. No Phase 1 compensating control is named.
findings.fix: Add a Phase 1 mitigation short of full Invariant 11: enforce a reserved-label prefix check at the benten-graph write-path layer (not just the evaluator), rejecting any WRITE whose target Node's label begins with 'system:' unless the write originates from the Engine's privileged API path (track via a write-context flag, e.g. WriteContext::is_privileged: bool set by grant_capability / create_view / revoke_capability and unset by user-operation WRITE). This is cheap (<50 LOC in benten-graph transaction primitive), testable ('tests/user_write_to_system_label_rejected'), and closes the forgery window until Invariant 11 lands properly. Add deliverable G3-A: write-context privilege flag + system-label rejection. Add test to Section 4.1 benten-graph: 'user_operation_cannot_write_system_labeled_node'. Add new error code E_SYSTEM_ZONE_WRITE to ERROR-CATALOG.md. Without this, the NoAuthBackend default is actively exploitable.

findings.location: Section 2.4 P3 + Section 2.6 N5 + Section 1 Exit Criterion
findings.claim: NoAuthBackend is shipped as the EngineBuilder default with zero guardrails against accidental production deployment. The plan says 'NoAuthBackend is the builder default' (N5) and 'Must be the out-of-the-box path — Phase 1 DX requires it' (P3). A Phase 1 developer who wires Engine::open() into a multi-tenant service, a networked API, or any context where untrusted input reaches registerSubgraph/call/transaction gets silent zero authorization. There is no deprecation-style warning on instantiation, no Engine::builder().production() constructor that refuses to build without an explicit capability policy, no telemetry marker, no logged notice. The plan does not require an explicit 'I accept zero auth' opt-in. This is the exact failure mode that produces headline breaches (e.g. Elasticsearch/MongoDB default-no-auth deployments). The CLAUDE.md three-pillar positioning names AI agents executing handlers with delegated authority as Phase 6; Phase 1's default posture predisposes every deployment toward a missing auth layer that is now load-bearing for agent safety. 'Zero-auth is correct for embedded/local-only' is a valid design point, but 'zero-auth is the default with no friction against network-exposed misuse' is not.
findings.evidence: Section 2.4 P3: 'Ship. Default backend; check_write returns Ok(()) unconditionally. Zero cost. | P1 | Must be the out-of-the-box path — Phase 1 DX requires it'. Section 2.6 N5: 'NoAuthBackend is the builder default.' Section 1 Exit Criterion step 3: 'Revoke the write capability, call post:create again, assert the response came through the subgraph's ON_DENIED edge' — the exit criterion tests capability denial, but requires the test to configure a non-default backend because NoAuthBackend grants everything. There is no test asserting 'default configuration produces a warning' or 'Engine::builder().production() refuses NoAuthBackend'. ENGINE-SPEC line 219: 'Enforced at the engine level (every WRITE checks capabilities before executing)' — the plan's Phase 1 default disables this.
findings.fix: Three-part remediation, all Phase 1: (a) Emit a single startup log line at info level when NoAuthBackend is in use: 'benten-engine: running with NoAuthBackend (no authorization). Acceptable for embedded/single-user; configure a CapabilityPolicy for multi-user/networked use.' Gated by tracing feature but fires through eprintln fallback if tracing is off so embedded-no-logger deployments still see it once. (b) Add Engine::builder().require_capability_policy() or ::production() that returns Err(EngineError::NoCapabilityPolicyConfigured) if called without explicit policy() — opt-in safer constructor for production users. (c) Add a README/QUICKSTART 'Security Posture' section naming NoAuthBackend's semantics explicitly. All three are cheap; (b) is 10 LOC in G7; (c) is prose. Add deliverables: N5-b 'production() builder method', T10 'docs/SECURITY-POSTURE.md'. Add test 'engine_builder_production_refuses_noauth'. This transforms the default from silent-dangerous to loud-documented.

findings.claim: Phase 1 has zero supply-chain security commitments despite being the foundation layer for a decentralized platform. The plan ships wasmtime, redb, BLAKE3, serde_ipld_dagcbor, napi-rs v3, tokio (transitively), mimalloc, papaya, ed25519-dalek, uhlc — every one a transitive-dep blast radius. The core2 yank during the phase-1-stack spike was a live supply-chain event within this very project's dependency graph. The plan adds zero controls in response: no 'cargo audit' / rustsec job in CI (T4-T9 cover multi-arch, MSRV, wasm-runtime, codegen drift, determinism — nothing on vulnerabilities), no cargo-deny license/ban policy, no Cargo.lock commit+verify policy spelled out, no SBOM generation, no pinning strategy (tilde vs caret), no dependency-update cadence, no documented response protocol if another transitive dep gets yanked mid-Phase-1. Section 5 Rank 10's scope-boundary list names network-fetch backends and SANDBOX as Phase 2 but does not name dependency-security as any phase. This is especially concerning because Phase 3 ships iroh (P2P), ssi, rs-ucan — adding crypto + networking transitive deps that will multiply the audit surface. Establishing the CI discipline now is ~100x cheaper than retrofitting after a yank/RUSTSEC advisory drops during G5 or G8.
findings.evidence: Section 2.8 Developer Tooling lists T1-T9 deliverables; none cover vulnerability scanning. Section 4.6 Cross-cutting harnesses covers determinism, error-catalog drift, cross-leg byte equality — no rustsec, no cargo-audit, no cargo-deny. Section 5 risks list 10 items; supply-chain not among them. SPIKE-phase-1-stack-RESULTS referenced in plan line 6 documents core2 yank mitigation but plan carries no forward-looking commitment. CLAUDE.md line 1 tech-stack lists 15+ crates, each a supply-chain edge.
findings.fix: Add Phase 1 deliverable T10 'Supply-chain CI': (a) cargo-audit or cargo-deny step in CI on every PR, failing on HIGH/CRITICAL RUSTSEC advisories. (b) cargo-deny.toml pinning allowed licenses (MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD-3/CC0) and banning known-problematic crates. (c) Cargo.lock committed and CI verifies 'cargo build --locked' produces identical lockfile. (d) Documented response protocol in CONTRIBUTING.md: 'If a dep is yanked mid-phase, open a tracking issue, pin-by-hash in Cargo.lock if upstream is slow, block the PR merge until resolved.' (e) Schedule a weekly 'cargo update --dry-run' + 'cargo audit' CI workflow that opens an issue on new advisories. Total cost: one CI file + one toml + one CONTRIBUTING section. Adds one row to Appendix A. Without this, the first supply-chain event after Phase 1 ships will be found by users, not CI.

findings.location: Section 2.4 P6 + Section 2.5 E3 + ENGINE-SPEC line 29
findings.claim: The `requires` property on a handler Node is the sole mechanism for capability enforcement (P6: 'When a handler Node has a requires: store:post:write property, the evaluator consults the capability policy before executing. This is the GATE-as-property rule'). ENGINE-SPEC line 29 confirms 'The requires property on any Node is checked automatically by the evaluator; capability denial routes to ON_DENIED edges.' But the plan does not specify — anywhere — that the evaluator ALSO checks the capability against the actual operations the handler performs (the concrete set of WRITE targets, CALL targets, SANDBOX invocations). A user who controls subgraph registration via registerSubgraph(sg) from napi can author a handler with `requires: "trivial:read:public"` that internally performs WRITE operations against admin-labeled Nodes, CALLs into privileged handlers with isolated:false attenuation, or touches IVM views requiring ivm:createView. If the capability hook is consulted based on the declared `requires` string (rather than the operations-actually-performed), the attacker gets admin writes authorized by a trivial grant. The plan's P6 prose reads as declared-property-based; E3 typed error edges include ON_DENIED but don't specify the denial source. This is the archetypal declare-vs-enforce security bug.
findings.evidence: Section 2.4 P6: 'When a handler Node has a requires: store:post:write property, the evaluator consults the capability policy before executing.' Section 2.5 E3: '(ON_NOT_FOUND, ON_EMPTY, ON_CONFLICT, ON_DENIED, ON_ERROR, ON_LIMIT)' — no specification of which ON_DENIED source fires. ENGINE-SPEC line 29 (parenthetical): 'The requires property on any Node is checked automatically by the evaluator' — 'is checked' is ambiguous between 'the declared value is used as the auth request' and 'operations are checked against the declared value'. Section 4.1 benten-caps tests: 'requires property triggers the hook with the correct context' — passes with either interpretation, does not assert the hook sees actual operations. Section 4.3 integration tests: 'Capability-gated CRUD round-trip: register crud('post'), grant a capability, engine.call('post:create', …) succeeds' — uses crud() which sets requires correctly; no adversarial test.
findings.fix: Resolve ambiguity explicitly in the plan + add adversarial tests. (a) State in P6: 'The requires property declares the minimum capability the handler needs; the evaluator ALSO checks each primitive's effective capability requirement at call-time (WRITE against write:<label>, CALL-isolated-false against the callee's requires, etc.). A handler whose actual operations exceed its declared requires has the excess operations denied individually.' (b) Add Phase 1 test to Section 4.1 benten-eval: 'handler_with_understated_requires_denies_excess_writes' — register a handler with requires: 'post:read' that internally performs a WRITE; assert the WRITE returns E_CAP_DENIED even though the declared requires grant was present. (c) Add 'handler_cannot_escalate_via_call_attenuation' — CALL with isolated:false must not widen capabilities beyond declared requires. These tests are load-bearing for Phase 6 (AI agents with delegated authority) — an AI-authored handler lying about requires is the canonical attack. If (a) is false and the evaluator only checks declared-requires, this is a wontfix-for-Phase-1 critical that the plan must name honestly so Phase 3/6 aren't built on a broken assumption.

findings.claim: The plan defers Invariant 13 (TOCTOU protection) to Phase 2 but does not document the Phase 1 TOCTOU window size or make it testable. P1 says 'TOCTOU check is Phase 2 (invariant 13); Phase 1 checks at commit per ENGINE-SPEC §9.' Commit-time check is better than op-time check for intra-transaction atomicity, but a handler evaluation spans multiple transactions (one per CALL boundary? unclear from the plan), and nothing prevents a capability from being revoked via Engine::revoke_capability between op N and op N+1 of a single handler — if the cap was cached at evaluation start, the revoked cap still authorizes the write. The plan does not specify (i) when the capability is read from the graph during evaluation, (ii) whether cached caps are re-fetched across CALL boundaries, (iii) whether a revocation during a long-running ITERATE is reflected mid-loop. Phase 1 ships handlers that can iterate 100K+ times per ENGINE-SPEC §4 invariant 8; a cap cached for that duration has a multi-second revocation window where denied writes land. This is particularly dangerous for Phase 3+ sync where a peer's revocation message arrives asynchronously.
findings.evidence: Section 2.4 P1: 'TOCTOU check is Phase 2 (invariant 13); Phase 1 checks at commit per ENGINE-SPEC §9'. ENGINE-SPEC line 229: 'Capabilities are checked at transaction commit time, not at individual operation time. This ensures atomicity (the entire transaction reflects the capability state at commit) and avoids TOCTOU windows where a capability is revoked between two operations in the same transaction.' — this covers intra-transaction atomicity but says nothing about cross-transaction (CALL boundary) or cross-iteration (ITERATE over revocation). Section 4.1 benten-caps tests: 'check_write_called_at_commit' — asserts the timing but not the freshness. No test named 'capability_revocation_during_iteration_denies_subsequent_writes'.
findings.fix: (a) Document Phase 1 TOCTOU window explicitly in a new Section 2.4 P1 note: 'The capability snapshot is refreshed at every commit boundary (every transactional: true group, every CALL entry, every ITERATE batch boundary defined as N iterations where N is configurable per capability grant, default 100). Revocations between refresh points are not visible to in-flight evaluations. Phase 2 Invariant 13 reduces this window to zero.' (b) Add test 'capability_revoked_mid_iteration_denies_subsequent_batches' to Section 4.1. (c) Add error code E_CAP_REVOKED_MID_EVAL to ERROR-CATALOG (distinct from the pre-existing E_CAP_REVOKED for sync). (d) Name the TOCTOU window as an escalation item in Section 5 Rank 10. Making the window explicit lets a Phase 1 operator reason about it; leaving it implicit means Phase 3+ sync-revocation races will be latent bugs.

findings.claim: The plan does not address the E_NOT_FOUND vs E_CAP_DENIED distinguishability problem. If an unauthorized caller issues Engine::get_node(cid) for a Node they lack read capability on, does the engine return E_NOT_FOUND (privacy-preserving: does not confirm existence) or E_CAP_DENIED (honest about the reason but leaks that the Node exists to unauthorized callers)? The plan's typed-error-edge set (ON_NOT_FOUND, ON_DENIED in E3) makes these distinct at the evaluator level, and Section 4.3 integration test 'Capability-gated CRUD round-trip' asserts E_CAP_DENIED routes to ON_DENIED on the authorized-to-query-but-denied case. But nothing in Phase 1 covers the unauthorized-to-query case. In Phase 3+ sync this matters concretely: a peer probing for the existence of a Node by CID (e.g. confirming a specific document exists in a neighbor's graph) should not distinguish 'not stored here' from 'stored but not readable by you'. Establishing the semantics now — before the bindings solidify and before sync ships — is far cheaper than retrofitting.
findings.evidence: Section 4.3 integration test: 'Capability-gated CRUD round-trip: register crud('post'), grant a capability, engine.call('post:create', …) succeeds; revoke, same call returns E_CAP_DENIED routed via ON_DENIED.' — this is the WRITE case; no READ-with-revoked-cap test. ERROR-CATALOG.md E_CAP_DENIED entry: 'Message: Capability {required} not granted to {entity} for WRITE on {target}' — message is specific to WRITE, no READ variant. Section 2.5 E3 primitive list includes READ; no specification of how READ handles denial vs nonexistence. ENGINE-SPEC.md does not address this.
findings.fix: Decide and document the semantics in Phase 1: option (A) 'READ on a denied resource returns E_CAP_DENIED (leaks existence, honest error)' — simple, easy to reason about. Option (B) 'READ on a denied resource returns E_NOT_FOUND (privacy-preserving, masks existence)' — defensible but requires care about timing-side-channel (a Node exists but denied takes longer to check than one that truly doesn't exist). Option (C) 'Configurable per capability grant: grants carry an existence_visibility: visible|hidden flag.' Recommendation: ship (A) in Phase 1 with a named limitation, plan (C) for Phase 3 sync. Add to ERROR-CATALOG.md an E_CAP_DENIED_READ variant with context. Add test to Section 4.1 benten-caps: 'read_denied_returns_cap_denied_not_not_found' (or whichever variant you ship). Add note to Section 2.4 that Phase 3 sync reviewers revisit this with hiding-existence as an option. Without choosing, R5 implementers will pick at random and the semantics will drift per call site.

findings.claim: The napi bindings (B3-B7) accept structured JS objects across the TS-Rust boundary with zero documented input-validation limits. Rank 8 recommends 'structured JS object for Phase 1 ergonomics; the napi binding validates and canonicalizes on arrival' but does not enumerate what validation covers. Specific attack vectors the plan does not name: (i) Value::Map with millions of keys (memory exhaustion during DAG-CBOR canonicalization); (ii) deeply nested Value::List/Map (stack overflow during recursive serialization — note the plan claims the evaluator is iterative but the Value serializer is almost certainly recursive); (iii) Value::Bytes with multi-GB payload (allocated into Rust Vec<u8> before any size check, potential OOM); (iv) CID with wrong multicodec or wrong hash length (must return a specific error, not parse-panic); (v) subgraph with well-formed but adversarially-sized structural-invariant-violations (4096 nodes × 8192 edges × deep nesting — invariants 5/6 cover this but only at registration; the napi boundary should reject early before allocating the full subgraph into Rust); (vi) malformed DAG-CBOR in subgraph-bytes form (if Rank 8 chooses bytes-wire-format in the future); (vii) recursive UTF-8 edge cases in Value::Text that break string-length bounds. Phase 1 hardens this surface forever — AI agents (Phase 6) will be the primary caller of these bindings with adversarial/buggy inputs.
findings.evidence: Section 2.7 B3-B7: all binding deliverables listed without size/depth/type-validation specifics. Section 5 Rank 8 recommendation: 'structured JS object ... the napi binding validates and canonicalizes on arrival' — the word 'validates' is not decomposed. Section 4.1 bindings/napi test line: 'every exposed function has a TS → Rust → TS round-trip test' — round-trip tests do not exercise adversarial inputs. ENGINE-SPEC invariant 5 (max 4096 nodes) and 6 (max 8192 edges) are registration-time invariants, not napi-boundary invariants.
findings.fix: Add deliverable B8 'napi input validation' in Section 2.7: enumerate the validation matrix — (i) Value::Map size ≤ 10K keys default, configurable; (ii) recursion depth ≤ 128 during Value deserialization; (iii) Value::Bytes length ≤ 16MB default, configurable; (iv) Value::Text length ≤ 1MB default; (v) CID parsing via the cid crate with rejected-multicodec tests; (vi) subgraph byte-length ≤ 1MB pre-parse; (vii) invariants 5/6 checked at the napi boundary before allocating the subgraph into Rust (fail-fast). Map each rejection to a new error code E_INPUT_LIMIT with context. Add tests to Section 4.1 bindings/napi: 'napi_rejects_oversized_value_map', 'napi_rejects_deep_nested_value', 'napi_rejects_oversized_bytes', 'napi_rejects_malformed_cid'. These are cheap (~200 LOC) and foundational — Phase 6 AI agents will exercise every one of these paths.

findings.claim: TRANSFORM expression sandboxing asserts 'No closures, no this, no imports, no prototype access' but does not enumerate the rejection-test matrix or address lesser-known JS escape hatches. The plan's recommendation to 'hand-roll a Pratt parser with a published BNF' (Rank 4) is structurally sound but the completeness of the grammar's negative space is what matters for security. Escape hatches not named in the plan: (i) 'new Function(source)' — runtime code compilation that most JS parsers tokenize as identifier+call; (ii) Tagged template literals 'fn\`template \${expr}\`' — invokes fn with raw+interpolated strings, a channel for smuggling operations past a 'no function calls' filter if templates are naively allowed; (iii) Optional-chaining-with-call 'obj?.method?.()' that could expose prototype methods (Array.prototype.map.call(...)) if property access is too permissive; (iv) Computed property names '{[expr]: value}' in object construction — the plan says 'object construction' is allowed; computed keys let you construct __proto__ or Symbol.iterator or constructor; (v) 'with' statement (mostly deprecated but grammatically legal); (vi) Destructuring patterns that evaluate getters; (vii) Numeric-coercion via template strings. Section 4.1 benten-eval says 'a rejection test per forbidden construct (closures, this, imports, prototype access)' — four constructs, not the ~15 class list. If the grammar is a positive-allowlist (only named tokens accepted, everything else is a parse error) this is defensible but the plan does not state allowlist vs denylist and the E4 'Subset of JavaScript' framing leans denylist-shaped.
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 E4: 'No closures, no this, no imports, no prototype access.' — four exclusions. Section 4.1 benten-eval: 'a rejection test per forbidden construct (closures, this, imports, prototype access)' — same four. Rank 4 names the parser choice but not the grammar's completeness properties. ENGINE-SPEC.md §15 Open Question 1 is cited but not excerpted — parsing-subset-completeness is not in Phase 1's observable surface.
findings.fix: (a) State explicitly in E4: 'Grammar is allowlist: every production is positively defined in the BNF; any token/AST shape not in the BNF is a parse error with E_TRANSFORM_SYNTAX. This includes new/Function/import/require/with/eval/yield/async/await/Symbol/Reflect/Proxy.' (b) Expand Section 4.1's rejection test list to the full class: closures, this, imports, prototype access (__proto__, constructor, prototype), tagged templates, template literals with expressions, optional-chained calls exposing prototype, computed property names resolving to __proto__/constructor/Symbol.*, 'new' keyword in any position, 'with' statement, destructuring with getters, spread-into-function-call, comma operator. Each should be one-line test. (c) Publish the BNF as docs/TRANSFORM-GRAMMAR.md per Rank 4 recommendation, with a 'Rejected constructs' appendix. (d) Add fuzz-test harness running generated JS snippets through the TRANSFORM parser and asserting every accepted string evaluates deterministically (no I/O, no prototype touches). This is especially important because AI agents (Phase 6) will author TRANSFORM expressions.

findings.location: Section 2.5 E5 + Section 5 Rank 10 + ENGINE-SPEC Invariant 14
findings.claim: The plan defers Invariant 14 (causal attribution — unsuppressible audit trail) to Phase 2 but Phase 1 is exactly when the 'AI agent executes handler' flows begin to be tested end-to-end (trace() per N3 is the Phase 1 debug tool, and the three-pillar positioning makes AI-assistant-as-adoption-driver Phase 6). By Phase 2 the system has already shipped with no provenance of 'who registered this subgraph, what caused this WRITE, which capability authorized this evaluation'. Phase 2 retrofit is more expensive than 'record the minimum attribution in the ChangeEvent and TraceStep today'. Specifically: ChangeEvent (G7) currently carries {cid, label, kind, tx_id} — no actor field, no originating-handler-cid, no capability-grant-id-used. TraceStep (E8) carries {node_cid, inputs, outputs, duration_us, error} — no actor, no capability-grant-used. An auditor in Phase 1 cannot answer 'which capability grant authorized this write' even though the information was in memory 2ms earlier.
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 E5: 'Invariants 4, 7, 8, 11, 13, 14 are Phase 2'. Section 5 Rank 10 lists Invariant 14 as Phase 2 escalation. ENGINE-SPEC.md line 108: 'Causal attribution on every evaluation | Unsuppressible audit trail'. Section 2.2 G7 ChangeEvent shape: '{cid, label, kind: Created|Updated|Deleted, tx_id}'. Section 2.5 E8 TraceStep shape: '{node_cid, inputs, outputs, duration_us, error}'. CLAUDE.md three-pillar positioning + Phase 6 AI assistants.
findings.fix: Expand both event types with minimum attribution fields in Phase 1, even though full Invariant 14 (cross-primitive unsuppressibility, sync-carried attribution) is Phase 2: (a) ChangeEvent gains {actor_cid: Option<Cid>, handler_cid: Option<Cid>, capability_grant_cid: Option<Cid>}. (b) TraceStep gains {actor_cid, capability_grant_cid_used}. (c) The evaluator populates these when it has them; NoAuthBackend populates actor_cid with a synthetic 'noauth:<session>' marker. (d) Add test 'change_event_carries_actor_when_policy_authorizes' and 'trace_step_names_cap_grant_for_write_operation'. Total cost: ~50 LOC across G3-A, G6-A, G6-C. Phase 2 Invariant 14 tightens this to 'required, not optional, and cryptographically attested' — Phase 1 just needs the fields present so Phase 2 isn't a schema migration. Without this, every Phase 1 deployment's audit logs are retroactively incomplete.

findings.claim: UCANBackend (P4) returns 'Err(CapError::NotImplemented)' unconditionally. This is correct for Phase 1, but the failure mode has three under-specified risks. (i) An operator who intentionally configures UCANBackend (reasonably believing it works based on the doc language 'UCAN-compatible, Phase 3') gets all writes denied with a generic 'NotImplemented' — the error message must explicitly direct them to Phase 3 + suggest NoAuthBackend or a custom policy as interim. (ii) The test 'ucan_stub_errors_cleanly' in Section 4.1 asserts cleanly-errors but does not assert the message content names the phase or the alternative — operator-hostile if left at 'not implemented'. (iii) More subtly: the plan relies on CapError::NotImplemented mapping to a distinct error code — if this code is accidentally catch-alled into 'policy denied' handling (E_CAP_DENIED), an operator who configures UCANBackend might see their write attempts showing up as capability-denial audit entries, not configuration-errors, and debug in the wrong direction. The plan does not reserve a distinct E_CAP_NOT_IMPLEMENTED code in ERROR-CATALOG.md.
findings.evidence: Section 2.4 P4: 'Type defined + check_write returns Err(CapError::NotImplemented). Full impl is Phase 3 benten-id. | P1 | Present so the trait shape is exercised against a second backend'. Section 4.1 benten-caps: 'UCAN stub cleanly errors with the right code'. ERROR-CATALOG.md E_CAP section includes E_CAP_DENIED, E_CAP_REVOKED, E_CAP_ATTENUATION — no E_CAP_NOT_IMPLEMENTED.
findings.fix: (a) Add E_CAP_NOT_IMPLEMENTED to ERROR-CATALOG.md with message: 'Capability backend {backend_name} does not implement check_write in this phase. UCANBackend is implemented in Phase 3. Configure NoAuthBackend for embedded/local-only use or provide a custom CapabilityPolicy impl.' (b) Update P4 deliverable: 'Returns Err(CapError::NotImplemented { phase: Phase3, alternative: NoAuthBackend })'. (c) Expand test to 'ucan_stub_error_message_names_phase_and_alternative'. (d) Add integration test confirming the error does NOT route to ON_DENIED (it routes to ON_ERROR with a distinct code) — this prevents the misdiagnosis risk. Trivial to implement, significantly better operator experience.

findings.claim: The error-catalog drift detector (T7) is specified but its failure mode is not. If the drift detector fails in CI, three scenarios are possible depending on implementation: (i) CI blocks the PR (correct — developer must sync catalog/Rust/TS). (ii) The Rust enum was hand-authored (C7) so it still compiles; the drift is only in codegenned TS — PR goes green in the Rust-only matrix, fails only in the JS workflow. Developer may merge from a stuck-on-Rust CI state. (iii) Worse: at runtime, a subgraph references an error code that exists in ERROR-CATALOG.md but was not yet added to the Rust enum — does the engine panic, emit a generic 'unknown code' error, or silently map to a default? The plan does not specify. This matters because ERROR-CATALOG.md is plain Markdown edited by any developer; the Rust enum is hand-authored (per T7 resolution). A PR that adds a new error code to ERROR-CATALOG.md but forgets to add it to the Rust enum will pass local cargo build (the enum doesn't know about it), pass cargo test (no test references it), but fail the drift detector — OR pass if the drift detector is TS-only.
findings.evidence: Section 2.8 T7: 'Decided pre-R1: Rust enum is the source of truth, hand-authored in G1-A (C7). T7 reads docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md + crates/benten-core/src/error_code.rs and (a) generates packages/engine/src/errors.ts from the catalog, and (b) runs a drift detector in CI that fails if any of the three (catalog doc, Rust enum, TS types) diverge.' Section 4.6 drift-detector bullet confirms 'fail on drift'. Neither spells out the CI-blocking behavior or the runtime semantics for unknown codes.
findings.fix: (a) State explicitly in T7: 'Drift detector is a required CI status check; merges are blocked until it passes. It compares (Catalog ↔ Rust enum ↔ TS types) bidirectionally — any absent-from-one triggers failure with a specific message naming the missing variant.' (b) Decide Rust enum behavior on encountering an unknown code at runtime (e.g. from a deserialized subgraph that references a future code): recommend ErrorCode::Unknown(String) fallback variant + test 'unknown_error_code_preserves_string_not_panic'. (c) Add a T7-adjacent test: 'drift_detector_fails_on_missing_rust_variant' (intentionally leave a code out of the enum, run the detector, assert exit code non-zero and error message names the code). This prevents silent skew during Phase 1 development when ERROR-CATALOG.md is edited frequently.

findings.claim: BLAKE3 provides 128-bit collision resistance (half the 256-bit output, standard cryptographic hash analysis). The plan uses CIDs as content-addressed identifiers throughout — any security property that relies on full 256-bit preimage resistance should be documented explicitly. Phase 1 itself has no such property (CIDs are used for deduplication and integrity, which both rely on collision-resistance not preimage-resistance). But Phase 3 UCAN capabilities may reference target resources by CID, and if a peer can find a preimage collision on a target CID they could forge capability matches. This is not a Phase 1 bug, it is a Phase 1 documentation gap that will surface later if unnamed now.
findings.evidence: Section 2.1 C4: 'Content hashing (BLAKE3 + DAG-CBOR + CIDv1)'. ENGINE-SPEC §7 (not read in full but §7 is content-addressing per plan context). Phase 3 scope per CLAUDE.md: 'UCAN-compatible, operator-configured'. CIDs appearing in capability grants (P2) are not named in any security-property document.
findings.fix: Add a one-paragraph note to ENGINE-SPEC §7 (or a new docs/SECURITY-PROPERTIES.md): 'BLAKE3 as used here provides ≥128-bit collision resistance and ≥256-bit preimage resistance under the standard model. No Phase 1 security property relies on full preimage resistance. Phase 3+ capability grants may reference target resources by CID; capability validation is secure against collision (Q(2^128)) attacks but should treat CIDs as capability-bearing identifiers — do not print adversary-controlled CIDs into logs as if they were authenticated.' Cost: prose. Add it now so the assumption is named before Phase 3 introduces UCAN.

findings.claim: Rank 2 resolves the change-stream channel type as 'bounded tokio broadcast with high-water mark → stale view on overflow'. This is correct for IVM latency but introduces a security-adjacent property the plan does not discuss: a fast writer can force IVM views stale by outpacing subscribers, which then routes reads to E_IVM_VIEW_STALE (a graceful degradation path). An adversary with write capability but not read capability on the views can use this as a denial-of-service vector against the read surface: flood with writes, keep all views stale, legitimate readers see stale errors indefinitely. Phase 1 is embedded/local-only so the threat model is limited, but Phase 3 sync extends the write surface to peers. Establishing a per-capability write-rate limit (or at minimum a metric exposed via trace) in Phase 1 lets Phase 3 harden without schema change.
findings.evidence: Section 5 Rank 2 recommendation: 'bounded tokio broadcast ... with a configurable high-water mark that, on overflow, marks affected views as stale.' Section 2.3 I8: 'each view has a hardcoded max-work-per-update budget; on exceed, mark view stale and emit E_IVM_VIEW_STALE. Async recompute is Phase 2.' No rate-limit on the write-side. ENGINE-SPEC.md performance target: 'Concurrent writers: 100-1000 writes/sec single-community ceiling'.
findings.fix: (a) Expose a metric (via the tracing feature flag) 'benten.ivm.view_stale_count{view_id}' so operators can detect view-flooding. (b) Add a Phase 1 open question to Section 5 Rank 10 or Rank 11: 'Per-capability write rate limit — Phase 1 records metrics, Phase 3 enforces per-peer rates.' (c) Minor test: 'ivm_view_stale_metric_increments_on_budget_exceed'. This preserves the option without costing Phase 1 much.

findings.claim: T8 adds 'cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p benten-napi' as a Phase 1 CI compile-check. Good. But the plan does not name the security implication of the 'same codebase → native + WASM' promise: the WASM target has fundamentally different trust properties (runs in a browser sandbox vs on a trusted host), which affects which parts of the capability/storage stack are meaningful. A NoAuthBackend shipped to a WASM target in a browser context means the browser page has full uncapped access to the user's graph — possibly fine for a single-user app, a security hole in an embedded-widget scenario. The compile-check proves structural compatibility but doesn't surface the different-trust-model question.
findings.evidence: Section 2.8 T8: 'Compile-check the full bindings/napi crate for wasm32 in CI. Proves the napi-rs v3 dual-target promise holds at the binding-crate level, not just for benten-core.' Section 5 Rank 4.5: 'Full WASM runtime with network-fetch backend remains Phase 2 per Rank 10.'
findings.fix: Add a note to T8 or to a new Phase 2 escalation in Rank 10: 'When the WASM runtime ships (Phase 2), the default capability backend for browser-hosted engines must NOT be NoAuthBackend — recommend a browser-specific BrowserOriginCapBackend that scopes writes to the originating origin. Phase 1 ships the compile-check; Phase 2 ships the browser-appropriate default.' Cost: one sentence. Prevents the trap of 'WASM just works' carrying NoAuth into browser contexts.

findings.claim: The test landscape is thorough for happy-path correctness but names zero adversarial/security-class tests by category. Section 4.1 covers unit tests per-primitive and per-invariant; Section 4.3 covers five integration scenarios — none labeled 'adversarial', 'fuzz', or 'security'. R3 dispatches 'rust-test-writer-security' as a parallel subagent (Section 6) but the plan does not seed it with categories. This leaves the security-test-writing agent to invent coverage from whole cloth in R3, when the plan-level list would be cheap to assemble now.
findings.evidence: Section 4.1 — 'unit tests mandatory coverage' — lists per-primitive happy path + per-invariant rejection + proptest. No adversarial. Section 4.3 integration: five scenarios, all correctness. Section 6 R3 includes 'rust-test-writer-security' with no seeded coverage list.
findings.fix: Seed Section 4.1 with a 'security-class tests' subsection naming (non-exhaustive): (i) napi oversized/malformed inputs rejected (cross-ref finding #7); (ii) TRANSFORM grammar rejection per forbidden-construct class (cross-ref #8); (iii) capability-revocation-during-iteration (cross-ref #5); (iv) handler-with-understated-requires (cross-ref #4); (v) user-write-to-system-label rejected (cross-ref #1); (vi) unauthorized-read-existence-visibility (cross-ref #6); (vii) ucan-stub-error-routes-to-ON_ERROR-not-ON_DENIED (cross-ref #10); (viii) supply-chain CI green (cross-ref #3). This gives rust-test-writer-security concrete targets in R3.
